series.title: [PATCH] migration/ram: avoid page population in ram_handle_zero

review.summary: The optimization is valid for anonymous/tmpfs-backed sparse RAM, but it changes guest-visible semantics for shared file-backed RAM. Replacing an explicit zeroing write with MADV_DONTNEED can resurrect existing backing-file contents instead of producing zeros, so the patch is not safe as written.

stages.A: Reviewed the intent and call sites. The patch changes zero-page handling in incoming migration and RDMA registration paths by preferring qemu_madvise(QEMU_MADV_DONTNEED) over reading/writing the mapping.
stages.B: Checked functional equivalence. The new fast path is only equivalent to zeroing for anonymous or implicitly-zero tmpfs-backed pages; it is not generally equivalent for shared file-backed mappings.
stages.C: Assessed backend and runtime safety. Gating on !(RAM_PREALLOC) does not distinguish anonymous/memfd scratch RAM from persistent or pre-populated file-backed RAM, so backend semantics can change.
stages.D: Build/checkpatch status is clean, but there is no evidence here of coverage for memory-backend-file/share=on or other non-memfd shared mappings where backing storage may already contain data.
stages.E: Recommendation: restrict the MADV_DONTNEED fast path to backends where discard is provably equivalent to guest-zeroing, or keep the old memset path for generic shared/file-backed RAM.

findings.title: MADV_DONTNEED is not equivalent to zeroing for shared file-backed RAM
findings.description: The new early-return path assumes discarding the page preserves the same guest-visible result as writing zeros. That is true for anonymous mappings and freshly-sized memfd/tmpfs pages, but not for generic MAP_SHARED file-backed RAM. On such backends, MADV_DONTNEED drops the page cache and later faults reload the file's existing contents; if the destination backing file already contains non-zero data, the guest will see stale bytes instead of zeros after migration. The current gate only excludes RAM_PREALLOC, which does not protect against memory-backend-file/share=on and similar cases.
findings.suggestion: Only use the discard fast path for RAM backends where dropping the mapping is guaranteed to materialize zeros on the next access, such as anonymous RAM or explicitly-identified memfd/tmpfs scratch backends. For generic shared/file-backed RAM, keep the existing zeroing behavior (or add a stronger backend capability check instead of using !RAM_PREALLOC).
findings.confidence_reason: This follows directly from Linux MAP_SHARED file-backed semantics: MADV_DONTNEED discards cached pages but does not overwrite backing storage, while the previous path explicitly zeroed memory.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
